infobox.value: and the **Stikine Highway**, as well as the **Kitimat Highway** from [Kitimat](/wiki/Kitimat "Kitimat") to [Terrace](/wiki/Terrace,_British_Columbia "Terrace, British Columbia"), is the northwestern-most highway in the [Canadian province](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada") of [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"). A scenic route through some of the province's most isolated areas,[[2]](#cite_note-2) the highway first gained designation as **British Columbia Highway 37** in the year 1975. At that time, its southern terminus was at the community of [New Hazelton](/wiki/New_Hazelton "New Hazelton") on the [BC Highway 16](/wiki/British_Columbia_Highway_16 "British Columbia Highway 16") (the [Yellowhead Highway](/wiki/Yellowhead_Highway "Yellowhead Highway")). In 1972, with the completion of a new bridge over the [Nass River](/wiki/Nass_River "Nass River"), the highway's Yellowhead junction was relocated to a point on Highway 16 just south of the site of [Kitwanga](/wiki/Kitwanga "Kitwanga"). Highway 37 was then extended south to Kitimat in 1986 [[3]](#cite_note-3) superseding what was then designated [Highway 25](/wiki/British_Columbia_Highway_25 "British Columbia Highway 25"). At the north end, the highway briefly stretches into the [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon"), becoming **Yukon Highway 37**.

|  | |
| |  |  |  | | --- | --- | --- | | ←  [Highway 35](/wiki/British_Columbia_Highway_35 "British Columbia Highway 35") | **BC** | →  [Highway 37A](/wiki/British_Columbia_Highway_37A "British Columbia Highway 37A") | | ←  [Hwy 11](/wiki/Yukon_Highway_11 "Yukon Highway 11") | **YT** | →  [Hwy 1](/wiki/Yukon_Highway_1 "Yukon Highway 1") | | |

The **Stewart–Cassiar Highway**, also known as the **Dease Lake Highway** and the **Stikine Highway**, as well as the **Kitimat Highway** from [Kitimat](/wiki/Kitimat "Kitimat") to [Terrace](/wiki/Terrace,_British_Columbia "Terrace, British Columbia"), is the northwestern-most highway in the [Canadian province](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada") of [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"). A scenic route through some of the province's most isolated areas,[[2]](#cite_note-2) the highway first gained designation as **British Columbia Highway 37** in the year 1975. At that time, its southern terminus was at the community of [New Hazelton](/wiki/New_Hazelton "New Hazelton") on the [BC Highway 16](/wiki/British_Columbia_Highway_16 "British Columbia Highway 16") (the [Yellowhead Highway](/wiki/Yellowhead_Highway "Yellowhead Highway")). In 1972, with the completion of a new bridge over the [Nass River](/wiki/Nass_River "Nass River"), the highway's Yellowhead junction was relocated to a point on Highway 16 just south of the site of [Kitwanga](/wiki/Kitwanga "Kitwanga"). Highway 37 was then extended south to Kitimat in 1986 [[3]](#cite_note-3) superseding what was then designated [Highway 25](/wiki/British_Columbia_Highway_25 "British Columbia Highway 25"). At the north end, the highway briefly stretches into the [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon"), becoming **Yukon Highway 37**.

## Route details

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stewart%E2%80%93Cassiar_Highway&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Route details")]

Highway 37 starts its 874 km (543 mi) journey in the south at Kitimat and 59 km (37 mi) north it reaches Terrace, where it merges onto the [Yellowhead Highway](/wiki/Yellowhead_Highway "Yellowhead Highway"). The Yellowhead coincides with Highway 37 east for 91 km (57 mi) to Kitwanga Junction, where the Yellowhead diverges east.

North of the Yellowhead's Kitwanga junction, Highway 37 travels 76 km (47 mi) to [Cranberry Junction](/w/index.php?title=Cranberry_Junction&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cranberry Junction (page does not exist)"), and then another 80 km (50 mi) north to [Meziadin Junction](/wiki/Meziadin_Junction "Meziadin Junction"), where Highway 37A begins and heads west via [Bear River Pass](/w/index.php?title=Bear_River_Pass&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bear River Pass (page does not exist)") to [Stewart](/wiki/Stewart,_British_Columbia "Stewart, British Columbia") and [Hyder, Alaska](/wiki/Hyder,_Alaska "Hyder, Alaska"). Highway 37 travels north through the [Skeena Mountains](/wiki/Skeena_Mountains "Skeena Mountains") for 333 km (207 mi) to the [Continental Divide](/wiki/Continental_Divide "Continental Divide") communities of [Eddontenajon](/wiki/Eddontenajon "Eddontenajon"), [Iskut](/wiki/Iskut "Iskut") and [Dease Lake](/wiki/Dease_Lake,_British_Columbia "Dease Lake, British Columbia"), which straddles the [Stikine](/wiki/Stikine_River "Stikine River") and [Dease River](/wiki/Dease_River "Dease River") basins. Another 116 km (72 mi) north and Highway 37 reaches [Jade City](/wiki/Jade_City,_British_Columbia "Jade City, British Columbia"), where a junction to the former [asbestos](/wiki/Asbestos "Asbestos")-mining community of [Cassiar](/wiki/Cassiar,_British_Columbia "Cassiar, British Columbia") is located. North of Jade City, Highway 37 travels another 120 km (75 mi) to its crossing of the 60th parallel into the [Yukon](/wiki/Yukon "Yukon") Territory, becoming Yukon Highway 37 and terminating at a junction with the [Alaska Highway](/wiki/Alaska_Highway "Alaska Highway") near [Upper Liard](/wiki/Upper_Liard "Upper Liard") just 3.4 km (2.1 mi) later. According to the British Columbia Ministry of Transportation, "Most of the route is hard surface (either pavement or sealcoat) with approximately 1 km (0.6 mi) of gravel."[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Stewart%E2%80%93Cassiar_Highway&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

The Highway 37 of today is the result of highway extension projects began in 1959.[[6]](#cite_note-harvey1-6)

Originally, a roadway extended south from the [Alaska Highway](/wiki/Alaska_Highway "Alaska Highway") to serve the [Cassiar mining district](/w/index.php?title=Cassiar_mining_district&action=edit&redlink=1 "Cassiar mining district (page does not exist)"), eventually reaching Dease Lake and joining a road to [Telegraph Creek](/wiki/Telegraph_Creek "Telegraph Creek") (sometimes referred to as [Highway 51](/w/index.php?title=British_Columbia_Highway_51&action=edit&redlink=1 "British Columbia Highway 51 (page does not exist)"), but not signed as such). To the south, logging roads extended north almost as far as Meziadin Junction.

By 1968, the route of what is now 37A extended past Meziadin Junction north. This was done to allow for [asbestos](/wiki/Asbestos "Asbestos") from Cassiar to be shipped to market via sea from [Stewart](/wiki/Stewart,_British_Columbia "Stewart, British Columbia").[[6]](#cite_note-harvey1-6) By the middle of 1972, only a few miles remained to be built between Meziadin Junction and [Iskut](/wiki/Iskut "Iskut"). Four bicyclists, whose journey from [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska") to [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana") was chronicled in a May 1973 [National Geographic](/wiki/National_Geographic_Society "National Geographic Society") article, braved the muddy gap.

Once this route was completed, travellers only had to contend with limited hours for using the logging roads south of Meziadin Junction, roads which were upgraded during the 1970s. The completion of a new bridge over the [Skeena River](/wiki/Skeena_River "Skeena River") at Kitwanga in Mid-November 1975 gave better access to the [Yellowhead Highway](/wiki/British_Columbia_Highway_16 "British Columbia Highway 16").[[7]](#cite_note-7) Work continued through the mid-to-late 70s to upgrade the stretch of highway.

The Highway 37 corridor is slated for infrastructure enhancements as resource extraction activities increase in the Northwest region of British Columbia. The Northwest Transmission Line was constructed by [BC Hydro](/wiki/BC_Hydro "BC Hydro") and runs from [Terrace, British Columbia](/wiki/Terrace,_British_Columbia "Terrace, British Columbia") to Bob Quinn Lake, largely paralleling the highway.[[8]](#cite_note-8)
